chunk_summary: Folate deficiency in adolescents requires careful management, prioritizing B12 exclusion or co-treatment to prevent neurologic injury. Treatment includes folic acid supplementation, dietary counseling, and monitoring for anemia resolution and risk factors.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of excluding or co-treating B12 deficiency before addressing folate deficiency.
Learn the recommended folic acid dosing protocols for adolescents with folate deficiency.
Recognize dietary and clinical risk factors for folate deficiency in adolescents.
Develop strategies for monitoring hematologic response and preventing recurrence through diet restoration.
key_facts: Folic acid treatment: 5 mg orally once daily for 4 months, followed by reassessment.
Safety precaution: Folate can mask B12-related neurologic injury while correcting anemia.
Dietary risk factors: Low intake of pulses, greens, or restrictive eating patterns.
Maintenance dosing: 5 mg every 1–7 days under supervision for chronic risk cases.
Monitoring: Repeat CBC to assess hematologic response and symptoms in multifactorial anemia cases.
reference_quote: Folate can correct anemia while allowing B12-related neurologic injury to progress. (Clinical Practice Guidelines: Micronutrient deficiency, RCH)
